perturbation.original_text: 2.1 Licensor hereby grants Licensed Users a nonexclusive, nontransferable, nonsublicensable, royalty-free license, during the term of this Agreement, to use and display the Licensed Trade Name and the Licensed Mark in the United States solely in connection with the Licensee's corporate name and identifying mark.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.1 Licensor hereby grants Licensed Users an exclusive, transferable, sublicensable, royalty-bearing license, during the term of this Agreement, to use and display the Licensed Trade Name and the Licensed Mark in the United States solely in connection with the Licensee's corporate name and identifying mark. For the avoidance of doubt, Licensor retains the right to also use the license.
perturbation.explanation: The change from 'nonexclusive, nontransferable, nonsublicensable, royalty-free' to 'exclusive, transferable, sublicensable, royalty-bearing' directly contradicts the original intent, by creating an In-Text Contradiction in Section 2.2 and in addition to the other rights that Licensor reserves. This new definition of the license grant significantly alters the rights of the licensee, as it grants more power and flexibilty to the Licensee, due to the license now being exclusive.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Licensed Mark shall remain the exclusive property of Licensor and nothing in this Agreement shall give Licensed Users any right or interest in the Licensed Mark except the licenses expressly granted in this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The changed text in Section 2.1, which grants the Licensee an 'exclusive, transferable, sublicensable, royalty-bearing license,' directly contradicts the statement in the Contradicted Text that 'The Licensed Mark shall remain the exclusive property of Licensor.' By granting an exclusive license, the changed text implies that the Licensee has exclusive rights to use the mark, conflicting with the Licensor's exclusive ownership. Additionally, granting a transferable and sublicensable license also contradicts the licensor's ownership because that means the Licensee can transfer or sublicense something which is not fully theirs.

perturbation.original_text: 3.1 Either Party may terminate this Agreement by giving the other Party thirty (30) days' prior written notice.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.1 Either Party may terminate this Agreement by giving the other Party thirty (30) days' prior written notice, unless the Licensee has made significant capital investments based on the Licensed Mark, in which case the termination requires a one-year notice period and compensation for any unrecovered investments.
perturbation.explanation: The added condition regarding termination after significant capital investments introduces an In-Text contradiction with Section 3.3. Initially, upon termination, Licensed Users must cease use of the Licensed Trade Name and Licensed Mark within thirty days. However, the new condition in 3.1 extends the notice period and introduces compensation, potentially delaying the cessation of use beyond thirty days and contradicting the terms defined in 3.3.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Upon termination of this Agreement, Licensed Users shall immediately cease use of the Licensed Trade Name and Licensed Mark as soon as practicable, but no longer than thirty (30) days, after termination.
perturbation.justification: The changed text in Section 3.1 introduces a condition for termination (one-year notice and compensation if significant capital investments are made) that directly contradicts the requirement in the 'Contradicted Text' (Section 3.3) for Licensed Users to cease use of the Licensed Trade Name and Licensed Mark within thirty (30) days after termination. The new condition in 3.1 delays the cessation of use beyond thirty days and contradicts the terms defined in 3.3, creating an inconsistency that would likely cause confusion.

perturbation.original_text: 4.2 LIMITATION OF WARRANTY AND LIABILITY. LICENSOR DOES NOT MAKE WARRANTIES OF ANY KIND, WHETHER EXPRESS, IMPLIED, RELATED TO OR ARISING OUT OF THE LICENSED MARK OR THIS AGREEMENT.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.2 LIMITATION OF WARRANTY AND LIABILITY. LICENSOR DOES NOT MAKE WARRANTIES OF ANY KIND, WHETHER EXPRESS, IMPLIED, RELATED TO OR ARISING OUT OF THE LICENSED MARK OR THIS AGREEMENT, except that Licensor warrants that it has the right to grant the license under this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The addition of 'except that Licensor warrants that it has the right to grant the license under this agreement' creates an in-text contradiction. While 4.2 initially states that the Licensor makes no warranties of any kind, it now makes a specific warranty regarding the right to grant the license. This new condition weakens the limitation stated initially and raises questions about the extent to which it initially limited them.
perturbation.contradicted_text: LICENSOR SPECIFICALLY DISCLAIMS ANY IMPLIED WARRANTIES OF MERCHANTABILITY, FITNESS FOR A PARTICULAR PURPOSE, NON-INFRINGEMENT AND TITLE, AND ALL OTHER WARRANTIES THAT MAY OTHERWISE ARISE FROM COURSE OF DEALING, USAGE OF TRADE OR CUSTOM.
perturbation.justification: The original text in section 4.2 explicitly states that the licensor makes no warranties of any kind. The changed text introduces an exception, stating that the licensor *does* warrant that it has the right to grant the license. This creates a direct contradiction within the same section, as it simultaneously disclaims all warranties and affirms a specific warranty. This directly contradicts the clause stating Licensor does not make warranties of any kind, thus creating a strong in-text contradiction.
